title: Maximum Building Height
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C folder with the minimum-memory champion. The recommended and fastest-runtime proposal is implemented in C++; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: The memory objective can sort the provided int** restriction pointer array in place, relax heights in the same storage, and use only scalar state. C has the smallest practical runtime baseline and no vector/object metadata for this proposal.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Best for the recommended/runtime champion, but vector<vector<int>> and an auxiliary pair vector are larger than the in-place C pointer-array approach.
Selected. It mutates the provided restriction rows in place, uses qsort on the pointer array, and stores only scalar loop state.
Native and memory-safe, but platform wrapper and slice metadata do not reduce the footprint below the direct C signature.
Slice headers, runtime metadata, and GC baseline are less attractive for minimum peak memory.
Managed object arrays and runtime overhead dominate the few scalars needed by the algorithm.
Object-heavy nested arrays and VM baselines are not competitive for a least-memory target.

variants.approachJustification: Sort restrictions in place, propagate caps from building 1 and the virtual right bound, then scan intervals using the same cone-intersection peak formula. The tail after the last real restriction is handled as a pure climb because the global n - 1 cap cannot be tighter than the left reachability cap.

reasoningSummary: The C variant keeps the two-pass restriction relaxation but avoids a compact copy of the points. It pays qsort comparator overhead to minimize auxiliary memory.
